planting.note: Sow seeds on the soil surface in early spring or fall in moist, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in shade to part shade; giant bellflower is one of the tallest and most imposing bellflowers and genuinely thrives in shade — native to the shaded, moist ravines and streamsides of northern Europe and western Asia. It self-sows freely in suitable conditions and may naturalize in woodland gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part to full shade; giant bellflower is the most shade-tolerant and one of the largest-flowered of the European bellflowers — a tall, imposing perennial native to shaded, moist, north-facing banks and woodland ravines from Scotland to Siberia, producing large, broadly-toothed, nettle-like leaves on stout stems up to 4–5 feet tall and in June–July, the most substantial, richest, most deeply colored flowers of any commonly grown bellflower. The individual flowers are large (1.5–2 inches), tubular, bell-shaped, and in the best forms a deeply saturated, rich violet-purple or near-cobalt blue — not the pale lavender of most campanulas but a genuinely strong, saturated, blue-purple. It is one of the few bellflowers that actively prefers shade, and in the garden it combines with hostas, ferns, astilbes, and woodland wildflowers as a tall, shaded-border perennial of real presence. The cultivar 'Brantwood' (rich violet-purple) and 'Gleaming' (pure white) are widely available. Self-sows in appropriate moist, shaded conditions to build a colony.

planting.note: Surface sow seeds in early spring or fall in moist, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in shade; nettle-leaf bellflower self-sows freely and can naturalize in woodland garden conditions. It has a history as a medicinal herb: the botanical name trachelium refers to the Greek word for throat (trachea) as it was used in folk medicine to treat sore throats.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part to full shade; nettle-leaf bellflower is a shade-tolerant European native of hedgerows, woodland edges, and scrub, producing coarsely toothed, roughly textured leaves reminiscent of stinging nettle (hence the common name, though unrelated and harmless) and in July–August, upright stems bearing clusters of large (1–1.5 inch), broadly cup-shaped, violet-blue to blue-purple flowers — somewhat more openly bell-shaped than C. latifolia and carried in more branching clusters. Like C. latifolia, it is genuinely shade-tolerant and thrives in the moist, lightly shaded conditions where most bellflowers decline. It was used historically in herbal medicine for throat ailments (trachelium derives from the Greek for throat). A traditional English hedgerow plant, it naturalizes readily in the semi-shaded woodland garden. The double-flowered cultivar 'Bernice' (fully double, lilac-blue flowers) is the most decorative available form. Self-sows reliably in moist, shaded situations and can be used to create naturalized colonies under deciduous shrubs and trees.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds (after cold stratification for 4–6 weeks) in early spring in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; tube white penstemon is short-lived (2–4 years) but self-sows to maintain colonies in lean, dry, open soils. It is native to glades, rocky prairies, and limestone-based habitats throughout the central and southeastern United States.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; tube white penstemon is a native prairie penstemon of the central and southeastern United States, unusual among penstemons in its pure-white flowers — most penstemons in this region are purple, lavender, or pink, making the clean, tubular-white flowers of this species distinctively different. The flowers are slightly longer and more narrowly tubular than many penstemon flowers (hence 'tubaeflorus,' meaning 'tube-flowered'), arranged in loose whorls on upright stems from late spring through early summer. A prairie and glade native adapted to the hot, dry, limestone-based soils common through the ozarks, glades, and limestone-prairie regions of the central states, it is more heat-tolerant than many penstemons and suited to zone 9 gardens in Texas. The white flowers attract native bumblebees, solitary bees, and various hummingbirds. It is relatively short-lived as an individual plant but self-sows in lean, open soil to maintain colonies. Combines naturally with prairie companions — grasses, black-eyed Susans, coreopsis, and native asters — in naturalistic prairie garden compositions.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring (cold stratify for 4–6 weeks) in moist to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; longsepal penstemon is one of the more shade-tolerant eastern penstemons and grows naturally along woodland edges, disturbed roadsides, and open glades. It is longer-lived than some penstemons but benefits from periodic division or reseeding to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; longsepal penstemon is a native eastern North American penstemon of woodland edges and disturbed areas from Ohio south to Georgia and west to Missouri — one of the more shade-tolerant penstemons in the eastern native plant palette, making it useful for semi-shaded positions where most western garden penstemons would decline. The flowers are rose-pink to lavender-pink with pale-pinkish-white interior tube and two pale guide lines on the interior of the corolla, typical of the eastern penstemon group. Larger and more robust in stem and leaf than many prairie penstemons. Blooming in May through June, it is visited by bumblebees and ruby-throated hummingbirds. Naturally adapted to the woodland-edge conditions typical of much of the eastern United States garden landscape — the open, moderately moist, slightly acidic conditions of old fields, woodland clearings, and roadsides. A valuable native plant for gardens wanting native penstemons that succeed in something other than full sun, dry, western conditions.

planting.note: Surface sow seeds (do not cover — need light to germinate) in moist, slightly acidic soil in spring; palespike lobelia grows naturally in a wide variety of open to semi-shaded habitats including dry roadsides, open woods, meadows, and prairies across most of eastern North America. It is shorter-lived than great blue lobelia (L. siphilitica) but self-sows readily to maintain colonies.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to average soil in full sun to part shade; palespike lobelia is one of the most widely distributed and ecologically adaptable native lobelias in eastern North America — native to dry roadsides and disturbed soils, open prairies, open woodlands, and rocky glades from Quebec to Florida and west to North Dakota. It produces erect, unbranched spikes bearing a dense, elongating raceme of small, pale-blue to nearly-white, two-lipped lobelia flowers from June through September — the flowers significantly smaller and paler than the showier great blue lobelia (L. siphilitica) or cardinal flower (L. cardinalis), but produced freely and over a very long season on a more adaptable and drought-tolerant plant. This adaptability to dry and disturbed conditions makes it useful in difficult sites where the more demanding, moisture-loving native lobelias would fail. It is visited by native bees and an important native plant for open-habitat gardens. Self-sows to maintain colonies in appropriate conditions. More drought-tolerant and sun-tolerant than great blue lobelia, and better suited to natural, low-maintenance naturalistic garden settings.
